summary: I was born in San Antonio, Texas, on February 8, 1921, in Bexar County. After serving in the Navy during World War II, I returned to San Antonio. It was my home before and after the war, and where my family lived.

Before I enlisted in the Navy, I was a student at the University of Texas at Austin. I had a paper route in the mornings while attending school there. Joining the military interrupted my college studies, but I adapted quickly to military life.

I chose to enlist in the United States Navy rather than wait to be drafted. I underwent training in Chicago, including two weeks in electronics radio material school. During the war, I served aboard a Landing Ship, Dock and participated in operations in the Mariana Islands and Okinawa.

My Navy training included two weeks in Chicago, where I spent the first week in class and the second week assisting the lab instructor. This was my only specialized training before deployment. Chicago was where I learned the radio electronics skills I used later in the war.

I served aboard a Landing Ship, Dock (LSD), specifically LSD number 820. This ship took part in the Mariana Islands and Okinawa operations. We were involved in cleaning up operations and landing troops during the invasion of Okinawa.

I was stationed in the Mariana Islands for about a week during the war, mainly involved in cleaning up operations. This was before we moved on to Okinawa. The Mariana Islands were an important staging area for the Pacific campaign.

I participated in the Battle of Okinawa in 1945, landing at Agushi Harbor with the 2nd Marine Division. I served as a radio operator and even acted as a sergeant for a day. The battle was intense, and I was involved in mop-up operations from Agushi to Naha and Aishima.

During the Okinawa landing, I carried a 45 caliber pistol along with my radio transceiver. It was part of my gear when I went down the rope ladder from the ship to the landing boat. The pistol was my main weapon during those operations.

While on Okinawa, I was assigned as a radio operator for the 2nd Marine Division during the landing at Agushi Harbor. I even acted as a sergeant for a day with them. I accompanied the marines all the way to Naha, the capital of Okinawa.

I went ashore with the 2nd Marine Division and traveled all the way to Naha, the capital city of Okinawa. This was part of the mop-up operations after the initial landing. Naha was a key location during the Battle of Okinawa.

During mop-up operations on Aishima, I was about 200 yards from a group that included Brigadier General and the war correspondent Ernie Pyle. Unfortunately, a Japanese soldier shot into the group and killed Ernie Pyle. We later erected a monument in his honor on the island.

After the war, I learned about the atomic bombing of Hiroshima, which led to Japan's surrender. We were near the area when the bomb was dropped, and I remember the celebrations on board the USS Missouri for the Japanese surrender. Hiroshima marked a turning point in the war.

entity: Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki
summary: The atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki in August 1945 were pivotal events during my service. After these bombings, Japan surrendered, and we participated in the surrender ceremonies aboard the USS Missouri. These events effectively ended the war in the Pacific.

I was aboard the USS Missouri during the Japanese surrender ceremony after the atomic bombings. The Missouri was the site where the formal surrender was signed. It was a significant moment marking the end of World War II.

After Okinawa, our ship headed to Kyushu in southern Japan. We were supposed to support invasion forces near three towns: Takasu, Fuji, and Kanoya. However, a storm forced us to stay close to shore, and eventually, I lost my ship there.

Takasu was one of the three towns near Kyushu where we were stationed during the final stages of the war. We stayed close to shore near Takasu due to a hurricane. It was part of the area where we awaited further orders.

Fuji, in Kagoshima, was one of the towns near where our ship was stationed in Kyushu. We were preparing for invasion forces in that area but were caught off guard by a storm. Fuji was part of the southern Japan operations I witnessed.

Kanoya was another town near Kyushu where we stayed during the war. We were caught in a hurricane and stayed close to shore near Kanoya. It was near here that my ship was broached and I eventually was discharged.

After being discharged, I walked to Kadena Air Base, which had only one plane, a C-46 Commando. I asked for a flight out of there and was flown to Buckner Bay. Kadena was my point of departure from the combat zone back towards home.
The C-46 Commando was the only plane at Kadena Air Base when I was discharged. I asked for a ride and was flown out on that plane to Buckner Bay. It was my first step in returning home after the war.
At Buckner Bay, I was able to get an LCVP, one of the assault boats used in the war. This boat took me to Buckner Bay from the island. It was part of my journey back after the war ended.

I met a colonel who mentioned possibly going to Kelly Air Force Base, where I worked before the war, and he referenced General Douglas MacArthur. MacArthur was a prominent figure during the occupation of Japan, and I was aware of his role during my service.

While walking back to Agushi Harbor, I encountered a 'Deuce and a Half,' a large army truck. I hoped for a ride but the black soldiers driving it, frustrated with their limited roles, drove off without me. It was an example of the racial tensions and roles within the military at the time.

After returning home, I was involved with the Veterans of Foreign Wars organization. It was part of my post-service life, connecting with other veterans and supporting those who served overseas. The VFW helped me stay connected to the military community.

I was also a member of the Reserve Officers Association after my military service. This organization helped me maintain ties with other officers and stay involved in military affairs. It was part of my life after active duty.

Before my Navy training, I was at Camp Walters, which was part of my early military experience. It was one of the places where I prepared for service. Camp Walters was a key training site during the war.

Texas City, Texas, was part of my home state experience. Although not directly mentioned in my military story, it was a familiar place during my life before and after the war. Texas City represents the broader Texas community I came from.

After the war, I arrived at Terminal Island in California, near San Francisco. It was there that I finally drew some pay, as I had not received any during my Navy service. Terminal Island was a key point in my transition back to civilian life.

San Francisco was near Terminal Island where I was discharged and received my pay after the war. It was a major port and processing area for returning servicemen like me. San Francisco marked the beginning of my return to civilian life.
